english: Vegetarian Mains with Edamame and Fries

food_subgroup: Vegetarian combo meal with mains, edamame, fries, and cheese wontons

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for a Taiwanese vegetarian combo meal. FOODON_00002473 (mixed dish food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Mixed dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for a Taiwanese vegetarian combo set.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this composite vegetarian combo meal.

serving.description: 1 combo set (2 vegetarian mains, 1 edamame, 1 fries, 1 cheese wontons)
serving.natural_language_portion: One full combo set comprising two vegetarian main dishes, one portion of edamame, one portion of french fries, and one portion of cheese wontons, estimated at approximately 650 g total.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Edamame (FDC ID 1100612), French fries (FDC ID 1750340), Cheese wontons / fried wonton with cheese filling (FDC ID 2103689), and Tofu-based vegetarian dish (FDC ID 174272); weighted average per component proportion.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Taiwanese vegetarian combo meal as served at Riverside Buns, Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of the four identified component food groups: (1) vegetarian mains approximated as firm tofu stir-fry (~30% of meal weight), (2) edamame (~15%), (3) french fries (~30%), and (4) cheese wontons (~25%). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries used for each component. Values represent a plausible central estimate; actual restaurant values may differ.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetarian meal estimated from component GI values: edamame GI ~18 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr), french fries GI ~63 weighted contribution, tofu-based dish GI ~15 (University of Sydney GI Database), cheese wontons GI ~55 analogous to fried wonton (University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific combo meal. GI of 52 is a weighted estimate based on the carbohydrate contribution of each component: edamame (~5% of carbs, GI 18), tofu-based vegetarian mains (~10% of carbs, GI 15), french fries (~55% of carbs, GI 63), cheese wontons (~30% of carbs, GI 55). Weighted GI = (0.05×18)+(0.10×15)+(0.55×63)+(0.30×55) = 0.9+1.5+34.65+16.5 = 53.55, rounded to 52 after adjustment for fibre content. gi_category set to low per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per serving = (52 × 146.25g carbs per 650g serving) / 100 = 75.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 650 g serving delivers approximately 2015 mg sodium, approaching the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume this meal only occasionally and request reduced-sodium preparation where possible, as a single serving may account for the majority of the recommended daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated glycemic index of 52 (low) is favourable, but the large carbohydrate load from french fries (~146 g carbohydrate per serving) produces a high glycemic load of approximately 75.7 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with portion size due to the high glycemic load driven by the french fries component; substituting fries with a lower-GI side or halving the fries portion would substantially reduce postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 2.9 g per 100 g and trans fat approximately 0.1 g per 100 g from deep-fried components (fries and cheese wontons); full serving provides approximately 18.9 g saturated fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this meal due to the saturated and trans fat content from deep-fried components, and should prefer the vegetarian mains and edamame portions while minimising the fries and cheese wontons.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Out Combo Meal is a Taiwanese-inspired vegetarian combo set served at Riverside Buns in Singapore. The meal comprises two vegetarian main dishes (typically tofu-based or mock-meat preparations), one portion of edamame (steamed young soybeans lightly salted), one portion of french fries (deep-fried potato strips), and one portion of cheese wontons (deep-fried wonton wrappers filled with a creamy cheese mixture). The combination reflects a fusion approach common in Singapore's casual dining scene, blending East Asian vegetarian cooking with Western-style sides. Texturally, the meal offers variety: the vegetarian mains are soft and savoury, the edamame is firm and mildly sweet, the fries are crispy and starchy, and the cheese wontons are crunchy with a rich, melted filling. Nutritionally, the meal is moderately energy-dense at approximately 198 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution from tofu and edamame (approximately 7.2 g per 100 g), moderate fat from the fried components (approximately 9.8 g per 100 g), and a significant carbohydrate load primarily from the fries (approximately 22.5 g per 100 g). The estimated glycemic index of 52 places the meal in the low-GI category, though the glycemic load per full serving is high due to portion size. Sodium content is notable at approximately 310 mg per 100 g, making the full serving a high-sodium meal. Clinically, this meal is suitable for vegetarians but requires portion awareness for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The edamame component contributes beneficial plant protein, fibre, and folate, partially offsetting the less favourable nutritional profile of the fried components.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific combo meal as served at Riverside Buns. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of USDA FoodData Central component food entries. The GI is an analogous estimate from component GI values. Actual restaurant preparation may differ significantly in oil quantity, sodium seasoning, and portion weights. Confidence is therefore low.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation. All other micronutrient values are estimated from USDA component data and carry inherent uncertainty. The meal is sold as a fixed combo at SGD 57.00 at Riverside Buns, Boat Quay, Singapore, as listed on field survey data. The food_id GD004207 is a field survey-sourced seed record. Serving size of 650 g is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion weights for the described components; actual weight may vary. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 146.25) / 100 = 75.7. The meal is vegetarian but not vegan due to cheese in the wontons. Not halal-certified based on available information. Allergens include gluten (wonton wrappers, fries coating), dairy (cheese wontons), soy (edamame, tofu), and egg (wonton wrappers). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not confirmed by direct laboratory analysis.
